pi_items.source: Industrial process documentation; quicklime production from limestone is a real, widely practiced industrial process
Stoichiometric calculation verification; standard input amount for limestone calcination calculations

Chemical stoichiometry: CaCO3 (100 g/mol) → CaO (56 g/mol) + CO2 (44 g/mol). For 1000 kg: 1000 × (56/100) = 560 kg. Verified via molar mass calculations using NIST values and rounded textbook values. Molar masses: CaCO3=100.087 g/mol, CaO=56.077 g/mol. See https://www.webqc.org/molecular-weight-of-caco3.html and https://www.webqc.org/molecular-weight-of-cao.html

Chemical stoichiometry and mass balance: CaCO3 (100) → CaO (56) + CO2 (44). For 1000 kg: 1000 × (44/100) = 440 kg. Verified via molar mass calculations. Molar mass CO2=44.009 g/mol. Mass balance: 1000 = 560 + 440. See https://www.webqc.org/molecular-weight-of-co2.html
Standard tolerance for stoichiometric calculations in chemistry; represents ±5% acceptable deviation from expected values, which is reasonable for industrial processes

pi_items.verified_value: USLCI is a real, publicly available database maintained by NREL and U.S. federal agencies since 2001, containing critically reviewed LCA data for U.S. materials and processes
OpenLCA is a professional, free, open-source Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) software developed by GreenDelta since 2006, commonly used for creating and analyzing LCA processes and databases

pi_items.source: Standard chemistry textbooks and NIST references; confirmed by web search results on limestone calcination. Requires heating to approximately 900-1000°C

privileged_info_summary: The task requires creating a stoichiometric process model in OpenLCA for quicklime production via limestone calcination (CaCO3 → CaO + CO2). All metadata values are chemically accurate: 1000 kg of limestone (CaCO3, molar mass 100.087 g/mol) produces exactly 560 kg of quicklime (CaO, molar mass 56.077 g/mol) and 440 kg of carbon dioxide (CO2, molar mass 44.009 g/mol), satisfying perfect mass balance. The process is based on real industrial chemistry, not synthetic data. The task operates within the USLCI (U.S. Life Cycle Inventory) database context, a real publicly maintained repository of LCA data. The 5% tolerance is a reasonable standard for stoichiometric calculations.
